_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-9 Poisoning/Overdose/Environmental Exposure (Revised 05/23/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured. NOTE: Opioid overdose covered in Section 1-9. This protocol covers non-opioid poisonings.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-9: Poisoning/Overdose/Environmental Exposure — MDHHS Approved 5/23/23, Reviewed 2023
purpose: For opioid overdose, refer to Opioid Overdose-Treatment Protocol (Section 1-9). This protocol covers general poisoning, specific antidotes, and environmental exposures including extrapyramidal/dystonic reactions, tricyclic antidepressant overdose, calcium channel blocker/beta blocker overdose, eye contamination, and envenomation.

sections.title: General Poisoning/Overdose Management
points: Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol
Pediatric patients younger than 14 years use MI MEDIC cards for medication dosage when available
Use proper PPE and prepare for decontamination if necessary
Remove clothing exposed to chemicals for dry decontamination and refer to Hazardous Contaminated Patient-Special Operations when indicated
Identify the substance and bring a sample, medication, or poison container with the patient only if it does not pose a rescuer risk

If altered mental status, respiratory distress, suspected opioid overdose, seizure, cyanide gas exposure, nerve agent exposure, or organophosphate exposure is present, use the corresponding protocol pathway
Alert the receiving hospital if the patient may present a HAZMAT risk
For inhalation exposures, provide high-flow oxygen
Obtain 12-lead ECG per MCA selection and monitor cardiac rhythm; treat dysrhythmias per the appropriate protocol

sections.title: Extrapyramidal/Dystonic Reaction
points: Signs: acute muscle spasm, neck twisting (torticollis), tongue/jaw muscle spasm, oculogyric crisis, limb rigidity — typically associated with phenothiazine antipsychotics or antiemetics (e.g., haloperidol, prochlorperazine, metoclopramide)
Administer diphenhydramine IV: Adults older than 14 years: 50 mg; Pediatrics younger than 14 years: 1 mg/kg, max 50 mg

sections.title: Tricyclic Antidepressant (TCA) Overdose
points: Signs: tachycardia and wide-complex QRS
Establish cardiac monitoring — treat dysrhythmias per appropriate protocol

For symptomatic tricyclic antidepressant ingestion, contact Medical Control for sodium bicarbonate: Adults older than 14 years: 50 mEq IV, repeat as needed per Medical Control; Pediatrics younger than 14 years: 1 mEq/kg IV, repeat as needed per Medical Control
Avoid physostigmine
Contact Medical Control

sections.title: Calcium Channel Blocker / Beta Blocker Overdose
points: Signs: bradycardia, hypotension, heart blocks, altered mental status, cardiac arrest
Establish cardiac monitoring — treat dysrhythmias per appropriate protocol
For symptomatic calcium channel blocker overdose, contact Medical Control and consider calcium chloride: Adults older than 14 years: 1 gm IV; Pediatrics younger than 14 years: 20 mg/kg IV, max 1 gm
For other specific medications in overdose, such as beta blockers, contact Medical Control for further guidance

sections.title: Eye Contamination
points: Irrigate continuously with normal saline, tap water, or bottled water if available for 15 minutes; attempt to continue en route or as directed by Medical Control
For alkali exposure, maintain continuous irrigation
If available per MCA selection, administer tetracaine 1–2 drops per eye every 5 minutes, maximum 5 doses, to facilitate irrigation
Ensure the patient does not rub the eye

sections.title: Snake/Spider/Animal Bites and Envenomation
points: Protect rescuers; bring in a spider, snake, or scorpion only if captured and contained or dead and safe to transport
Immobilize the affected extremity below the level of the heart with sling or loose wrapping
Remove constricting items from the affected extremity
Do NOT use ice, refrigerants, tourniquets, scalpels, or suction devices for snake bites
Initiate prompt transport; transport snake-bite patients to the closest facility
Monitor for systemic symptoms: hypotension, coagulopathy, neurotoxicity, tissue necrosis
Mark margins of erythema or edema with the time measured when present
Eastern Massasauga rattlesnake is the only venomous snake native to Michigan, but exotic venomous snakes may be encountered; obtain species information and antivenom if available on scene

mca_selections_required.description: MCA must indicate whether tetracaine 0.5% ophthalmic solution is authorized for eye contamination irrigation facilitation

key_drugs.name: Diphenhydramine (Extrapyramidal/Dystonic Reaction)
key_drugs.dose: Adults >14 years: 50 mg; Pediatrics <14 years: 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg)
key_drugs.indication: Extrapyramidal/dystonic reaction from antipsychotic or antiemetic medications
key_drugs.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity; narrow-angle glaucoma; use caution in infants
key_drugs.side_effects: Sedation, dry mouth, urinary retention, hypotension
key_drugs.notes: IV preferred if access available. IM if IV/IO not readily established.

key_drugs.name: Sodium Bicarbonate (TCA Overdose)
key_drugs.dose: Adults >14 years: 50 mEq IV; Pediatrics <14 years: 1 mEq/kg IV; repeat as needed per Medical Control
key_drugs.indication: Symptomatic tricyclic antidepressant ingestion with tachycardia or wide-complex QRS
key_drugs.contraindications: Hypochloremic alkalosis; use caution with severe pulmonary edema
key_drugs.notes: Contact Medical Control for administration and repeat dosing.

key_drugs.name: Calcium Chloride (CCB/Beta Blocker Overdose)
key_drugs.dose: Adults >14 years: 1 gm IV; Pediatrics <14 years: 20 mg/kg IV (max 1 gm)
key_drugs.indication: Calcium channel blocker overdose with bradycardia or hypotension
key_drugs.side_effects: Bradycardia if administered rapidly; tissue necrosis with extravasation
key_drugs.notes: Administer slowly. Monitor ECG during administration.

key_drugs.name: Tetracaine 0.5% Ophthalmic (Eye Contamination)
key_drugs.dose: 1–2 drops per eye every 5 minutes, maximum 5 doses
key_drugs.indication: Eye contamination — to facilitate irrigation by reducing pain
key_drugs.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ester-type local anesthetics
key_drugs.side_effects: Transient stinging; corneal toxicity with repeated doses
key_drugs.notes: Use to facilitate irrigation when available per MCA selection. Continue irrigation for 15 minutes or as directed by Medical Control.

deterioration_flags: Wide-complex QRS or tachycardia in suspected TCA overdose — contact Medical Control for sodium bicarbonate
Symptomatic calcium channel blocker overdose — contact Medical Control and consider calcium chloride
Seizure activity — airway management; contact Medical Control
Dystonic reaction unresponsive to diphenhydramine — contact Medical Control
Snake bite with systemic symptoms — prepare to manage airway and hypotension and use related respiratory distress, shock, and anaphylaxis pathways
scope_notes: Scene safety, decontamination, supportive care, Poison Control contact: all levels
Eye irrigation with saline: all levels
Diphenhydramine IV for dystonic reaction: Specialist skill
Sodium bicarbonate for TCA and calcium chloride for calcium channel blocker overdose: Specialist skill with Medical Control involvement where specified
Tetracaine ophthalmic: Specialist skill — per MCA selection
Cardiac monitoring and 12-lead ECG: Specialist skill
